paragraphs.body: I have known you since I was two years old; you have known me since your eyes opened; there is no one I have played with more; there is no one I have punched more; there is no one I have laughed with more; there is no one who makes me angrier; there no one can annoy me the way you can; no one understands me better; I don't remember what there was before you; maybe because I was too young to remember, or maybe because I cannot imagine a life without you; I do remember what it was like having you follow me everywhere I went; you liked the thing I like; you were Interested in the things I was interested in; I get annoyed sometimes why can't you do your own thing; but I love having you behind me even if I did not say it; we turned our blankets and pillows into forts; we had battles for the ages with Nerf guns and plastic swords; Invented a game that only the two of us could ever dream up; we played sports like in was game seven of the finals; and it seemed that every game we played would ultimately end with a fight; who won the game; did you get shot or did it miss; was it a goal or did you save it; did the ball go out of bounds; was it a foul or not; without fail, we fought about everything; we screamed about what the right call was; then we started swearing; then we started getting physical; then it got more physical; then I hit too hard, and you would cry; the fight would end, and there was a stop in the madnes;. Then boredom would set in; finally there was a moment of truce; It was never an apology; someone would crack a joke and make the other laugh; one would give the other a little shove; that is how we forgive; we punch, kick, scratch, and slap, yet we are still back at it the next minute, playing again; we knew that no matter how nasty the fight was, it could not break us; our bond is inseparable, and I am lucky to be a part of it; I have seen you grow up; you once were my shadow wherever I went; then at some moment, you found your own path; you step out of the shadow; it used to be me leading, and you followed; now you don't follow; you try to catch up; you challenged me the way no one has ever had; you pushed me to go faster so you would not catch up; that only pushes you to go even faster; you became more than my little brother; you became more than the kid who followed me everywhere I went; more than the kid I have the duty to protect; more than the kid who got the smaller piece of the cookie mom gave us to split; you are the competitive fire that makes me go; you have the trust that I give no one else; you see, the thing about me that no one else could even come close to understanding; you have seen me when I thought I could take on the world; you had seen me at my lowest moments when I wanted to shy away; you have always been there in whatever I do; this is why, no matter the reason, I'll be there; I don't care if it takes months, years, or even decades, if you need me, I will come; if you face something unbreakable, I help break it down; if you face something unyielding, I help make it yield; if you fall, we will fall together; you are my greatest competitor; you are my greatest friend, but no matter what the paths of life take us and how the world changes, you are still my brother before anything else.

annotations.text: it; we turned our blankets and pillows into forts; we had battles for the ages with Nerf guns and plastic swords;
annotations.comment: It's passages like these that really help to illustrate what your relationship has been like, specifically.
annotations.context_left: en if I did not say

annotations.comment: Here's an example of a place where stronger concrete and specific images could really benefit the overall strength of the writing.

paragraphs.body: • Good modeling of “Girl” essay. • It is close to the word count, but I would add more.

• Greatest Strengths: keeps the reader on a hook, good description of little brother, shows importance and value that he has to you • It is clear that the central focusing idea is you’re little brother and how important he is to you.
• The audience that would care about this is your brother and probably your parents as well.
• You address the “so what” question well. Describing specific moments with you’re brother and always having that “competitive fire” etc. It makes it clear why he is important to you.

• I highlighted the lines I thought where really good in green. These lines stuck out to me as very descriptive or very powerful. I’d like to know more of the setting of some of these lines like where exactly did your brotherly competitions take place etc.

• I think an underdeveloped part of this essay is a little bit in the beginning. You have a good hook, but it is extremely long. You don’t clarify you are talking about your little brother until pretty late in the essay. Maybe introduce him a little sooner.

• The essay progresses well naturally. It starts with the begging when you where young, talks about all the good moments you have had and have currently, and then talks about the future how you will always be there. Good progression.
• I highlighted good details in yellow.
• I highlighted where the essay is too vague in cyan.
• When it comes to the right “englishes”, I think most of it is good. It becomes more profound at the end though. Is that how you’d talk to your brother? For example using the word “yields”.

• I find the grammar, form, and tone to be really good in this. The way I read it was fun but also nostalgic and serious at the same time. So if that is what you where going for, then you did it well.

• Overall for revision, I think you need to add some detail to certain scenarios like the basketball games, pillow forts, and nerf fights, etc. Maybe work on language and introducing your little brother as your little brother earlier in the essay. Doing these things will also increase your word count which you need to do anyway.

annotations.comment: Hello, It is clear that the central focusing idea of this piece is the lifelong relationship between you and your brother. The greatest strength of this piece is the relentless attention you pay to your brother in this piece. What I mean is that you don’t veer off and tell stories about only you, and you don’t flood the piece with your own needs and values, but every detail serves to develop the relationship between the two of you. The words that stand out the most to me here, honestly, are the pronouns you choose at various times. You, I, and we are at the heart of everything. It is clear that you are both your own individual selves, but it is also clear that the two of you together—the we of it all—this is another kind of self entirely. It’s almost as though the two of you have a confluence which becomes a third thing on its own—that’s really cool to think about a relationship so important that it becomes a whole other thing that is greater than the sum of its parts. The audience of the piece is clearly your brother, and the purpose of the writing is to celebrate what he means to you and how he has contributed to creating the person you are today. I believe, however, that if you continue to develop this concrete and specific details of this piece, it could have a much broader audience and a greater, more far reaching purpose. For instance, there are moments when you rely on vagueness to the extent that certainly no other reader could clearly understand a particular situation, and, potentially, even you and your brother might think of different examples, given certain generalizations. If you make these moments more specific, a reader is going to have a clearer sense of who you are and why this writing matters. Beyond that, I wonder if there are other relationships you might describe that would reinforce what you all mean to each other. For instance, I was thinking of the expression the nut doesn’t fall far from the tree and about how two nuts from the same tree have to compete for sunlight and nutrients but ultimately benefit each other by loosening the soil and keeping invasive competitive species at bay. (It’s kind of a silly example, but its what popped into my mind.) Or are there other human relationships that come to mind? One thing that your writing does an excellent job of illustrating is the importance of healthy competition in the development of an individual into adulthood. I think it is a very common trait of parents these days to try to prevent kids from bickering, sparring, battling over who gets which half of a cookie. And, as a parent, I can say that most of us want our kids to get along. But preventing this kind of healthy competition means preventing children from practicing solving problems, practicing reconciliation, sure, but, also, practicing standing up for yourself and getting what you need and feel you deserve. Thanks for Sharing,
